title: Beyond energy prices: the ripple effects of Gulf supply disruptions
gist: ECB staff model two Strait of Hormuz disruption scenarios and find that physical supply shortages of Gulf energy and non-energy goods (fertilisers, helium, petrochemicals) create macro damage beyond the direct energy-price shock, hitting Asia hardest and making inflation more persistent than a pure oil-price spike would.

insights.claim: Gulf countries supply over 50% of total energy imports for Japan, South Korea and India, and around one-third for China and ASEAN economies, versus only around 10% for the euro area, the UK and the US.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: A Hormuz disruption is structurally an Asia-demand shock more than a euro-area/US shock; energy-import-dependent Asian economies and their currencies/equities are the most exposed pressure points to watch.
insights.quote: Gulf suppliers account for over 50% of total energy imports in Japan, South Korea and India, and around one-third in China and ASEAN economies.
insights.locator: Exposure is concentrated in Asia and energy-intensive sectors

insights.claim: Non-energy goods from Gulf countries make up less than 1% of total non-energy imports for most advanced and emerging economies, with India a notable exception.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Broad (non-energy) Gulf trade disruption is a second-order risk for almost everyone except India, so the primary macro channel to monitor remains energy, not industrial goods, outside India.
insights.locator: By contrast, risks from disrupted exports of industrial

insights.claim: Energy-intensive industries — petrochemicals, aluminium, fertilisers and semiconductors — are the most vulnerable sectors to Gulf supply disruption, with spillovers into manufacturing and processing through global production networks.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Investors in these specific input-heavy sectors face concentrated exposure to a Hormuz shock even if their home country's aggregate energy dependence on the Gulf looks moderate.

insights.claim: Because Asia is the global manufacturing hub, a decline in its industrial output from Gulf disruption could have a widespread knock-on impact on global supply chains.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: A Gulf shock transmits globally not just via oil prices but via Asian factory output — a channel that price-only energy hedges would miss.
insights.locator: Given the role of Asia as a global manufacturing hub

themes.theme: Hidden chokepoint commodities: helium, methanol, fertilisers

insights.claim: Disruption to Gulf supplies could remove around one-third of global helium production and one-fifth of global methanol production.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Helium and methanol are underappreciated single points of failure; a Hormuz closure could tighten these specific input markets sharply even though they are a tiny share of headline trade statistics — worth tracking spot prices/availability as an early-warning indicator.
insights.quote: disruption to Gulf supplies could remove around one-third of global helium production and one-fifth of global methanol production
insights.locator: For example, disruption to Gulf supplies could remove

insights.claim: A helium and methanol supply hit could severely affect downstream sectors such as semiconductors, aerospace and other industrial inputs, particularly in Asia.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Semiconductor and aerospace supply chains carry latent Gulf-linked geopolitical risk via feedstocks, not just via energy costs — relevant for chip fabs and aircraft manufacturers assessing input-security risk.
insights.locator: This could severely affect related sectors

insights.claim: For products where Gulf countries have a large global footprint — fertilisers, aluminium and petrochemicals — knock-on risks from bottlenecks in key industrial inputs remain substantial even though Gulf non-energy exports are a small share of overall trade.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Aggregate trade-share statistics understate risk in a handful of concentrated commodity markets; agricultural input costs (fertiliser) and industrial metal/chemical supply chains are worth flagging as tail-risk exposures.
insights.locator: So, for products where Gulf countries have a large global footprint

themes.theme: Quantified output at risk (static trade model)

insights.claim: Persistent input shortages of energy goods alone could put up to 3% of euro area production at risk, in the upper-bound (poor-substitution) case.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: 3% of euro area output is a material recession-magnitude risk from a single geopolitical channel, giving a concrete benchmark for stress-testing euro area growth forecasts.
insights.quote: our estimates show that persistent input shortages of energy goods alone could put up to 3% of euro area production at risk
insights.locator: This blog illustrates how sensitivity analysis

insights.claim: In the energy disruption scenario, the static model shows the largest production losses in South Korea (up to 11%), India (around 8%) and Japan (around 7%), followed by ASEAN economies (up to 5%), versus up to 3% for the euro area.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: South Korea stands out with by far the largest modeled output-at-risk (11%), making Korean industrials/exporters the most sensitive single-country equity/currency exposure to a Hormuz closure.
insights.locator: Energy disruptions pose the greatest macroeconomic risks

insights.claim: If producers can more easily substitute missing inputs, losses are much milder: euro area production losses fall to 0.4% in the energy disruption scenario and 0.6% in the broad disruption scenario, versus the upper-bound 3% figure.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: The realized damage from a Gulf shock hinges almost entirely on substitution elasticity/input flexibility (7x difference between best and worst case) — a key variable to track via firms' inventory and reserve buffer commentary.
insights.locator: for example, euro area production losses amount to 0.4%

insights.claim: The additional output damage from non-energy goods shortages (the broad scenario, on top of the energy-only scenario) is generally limited for most advanced economies but notably more pronounced for India.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: India is the outlier economy where non-energy Gulf trade (not just oil/gas) is itself a meaningful macro risk, distinct from every other major economy in the sample.
insights.locator: The additional damage due to shortages of non-energy goods

themes.theme: Growth and inflation dynamics — China worst hit, inflation stickier than growth hit

insights.claim: In the dynamic DSGE model's energy disruption scenario, China's GDP growth is projected to be 0.8 percentage points lower in 2026 and 1.1pp lower in 2027, versus 0.3pp lower in the euro area and 0.1pp lower in the US in both years.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: China's growth hit is roughly 3-4x the euro area's and 8-11x the US's, making Chinese domestic demand and China-exposed equities the most macro-sensitive single trade to a Hormuz energy shock.
insights.locator: In 2026 and 2027 GDP growth is projected to be 0.8 and 1.1 percentage points lower in China

insights.claim: Consumer price inflation in the energy disruption scenario is projected to peak in 2027 at 2.3 percentage points higher in China, 0.9pp higher in the euro area and 0.3pp higher in the United States.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: A Gulf disruption is disproportionately a China inflation/stagflation shock, which has implications for PBoC policy space versus the Fed/ECB, who face comparatively milder tradeoffs.
insights.locator: Consumer price inflation is also projected to be higher, peaking in 2027

insights.claim: Combining the energy and broad disruption scenarios, euro area GDP growth would fall by around 0.4 percentage points in 2026, China's by 0.9pp and the US's by 0.1pp; inflation would peak in 2027 at 1.3pp higher in the euro area, 2.6pp higher in China and 0.5pp higher in the US.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: The combined-scenario numbers are the article's headline stagflation stress-test figures for 2026-27 — useful direct inputs for scenario-weighting euro area and China growth/inflation forecasts.
insights.locator: A combination of the two disruption scenarios

insights.claim: While the GDP growth effect of the disruption partly unwinds after the initial loss, inflation remains persistently higher, with peak impacts in 2027 and inflation effects persisting through 2028.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: The inflation shock is more durable than the growth shock — implies central banks would face a longer tightening bias even after activity stabilizes, a relevant asymmetry for rate-path positioning.
insights.quote: While the effect on economic activity partly unwinds after the initial loss, inflation remains persistently higher.
insights.locator: The peak impacts on GDP and inflation would occur in 2027

insights.claim: The vast majority of the inflation effects in the disruption scenarios comes from supply disruptions to non-energy import sectors rather than the direct impact of higher energy prices, meaning the added inflation is mainly transmitted through an indirect cost channel.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Contrarian-ish nuance: the extra inflation risk from a Hormuz closure is less about the oil price line item and more about hard-to-substitute intermediate-input costs feeding through supply chains — meaning inflation hedges tied purely to energy prices would under-hedge this risk.
insights.locator: Concerns over double-counting of price effects

insights.claim: The risks modeled would be larger if disruptions proved persistent, particularly if countries depleted energy reserves and firms faced tighter constraints in sourcing critical inputs.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: The scenario damage is a lower bound conditional on buffers holding; a prolonged closure that exhausts strategic reserves would push outcomes toward the worse end of the modeled ranges — a reason to watch reported reserve drawdown rates during any future closure.
insights.locator: These risks would be larger should disruptions prove persistent

insights.claim: The ECB authors argue that monitoring direct energy exposure alone is insufficient; institutions should also track indirect supply-chain dependencies, preserve adequate buffers, and strengthen contingency planning for sectors reliant on hard-to-substitute upstream inputs.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: A direct policy/corporate takeaway: firms and policymakers should build risk dashboards around upstream input concentration (e.g., helium, fertiliser, methanol supply chains), not just oil price exposure.
insights.locator: The analysis therefore points to the importance

insights.claim: Despite recent easing of Middle East tensions reducing the near-term likelihood of the specific Gulf disruption scenario, the ECB frames the exercise as a durable methodology for assessing geopolitical risk beyond direct energy-price and uncertainty effects.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: The ECB is signaling this is now a standing analytical framework it will likely reuse for future geopolitical shocks (Taiwan Strait, Russia-related, etc.), not a one-off note — worth watching for follow-on ECB scenario work.
insights.locator: Although the recent easing of tensions reduces the likelihood
